He conducted two rounds of research on influential figures in agentic DevOps as of March 2026, with the second being a deep research pass specifically oriented toward building an interview target list for thought leadership content.

The first research pass produced a general overview of influential figures including Thomas Dohmke (founder of Entire, former GitHub CEO), Mitch Ashley (Futurum Group analyst), Emilio Salvador (GitLab VP), and others, organized into tiers. The second, deeper research pass was scoped specifically to identify people Michael could realistically interview, with clarifying questions establishing the purpose as building an interview list. The deep research produced a comprehensive 23-person report organized into five categories: founders/CEOs building agentic DevOps tools (Solomon Hykes/Dagger, Amit Eyal Govrin/Kubiya, Spiros Xanthos/Resolve AI, Jyoti Bansal/Harness, Guy Podjarny/Tessl, Kumar Chivukula/Opsera, Adam Jacob/System Initiative), analysts shaping the narrative (Mitch Ashley/Futurum, Diego Lo Giudice/Forrester, Helen Beal/VSMC, Darryl K. Taft/The New Stack), senior practitioners with hands-on experience (Joseph Sandoval/Adobe, Vijay Samuel/eBay, Srikanth Rajan/Salesforce, Rickey Zachary/Thoughtworks), developer advocates and community voices (Viktor Farcic/Upbound, Bret Fisher/Agentic DevOps podcast, Patrick Debois/Tessl, Engin Diri/Pulumi), and open source maintainers (David Soria Parra/MCP at Anthropic, Joe Duffy/Pulumi, Idit Levine/Solo.io, Alexander Matyushentsev/Akuity).

Each profile includes current role, specific 2025–2026 output, public presence with social handles and podcast appearances, unique thesis on agentic DevOps, and a reachability rating (High/Medium). The report also includes five strategic interview themes and a recommended outreach priority sequence starting with Bret Fisher and Viktor Farcic as natural collaboration partners, then moving to founder-tier guests, then practitioners for credibility, and finally prestige targets like Patrick Debois and David Soria Parra. Kelsey Hightower is noted as a dream booking. The research was cross-checked across DevOps.com, The New Stack, Futurum Group, DEVOPSdigest, KubeCon speaker lists, CNCF community, GitHub/GitLab blogs, Pulumi blogs, LinkedIn, and Twitter/X.

content.title: 5 Key Trends Shaping Agentic Development in 2026 - The New Stack
content.text:
This year I looked at two apps (Conductor and Verdent AI) that explicitly support running tasks in parallel — that is, effectively defining a task and just leaving an LLM to execute it in the background while starting a new task. While you can generally ask any Agentic CLI to run a task in the background, more apps will support parallel running as a workflow in 2026. One thing to note about executing these: to work on the same codebase, the target code needs to be isolated. This usually means making a new branch for each task and then putting the code into a fresh folder, which is what git worktrees does. You then merge the work back into the main branch. All this git fiddling plays slightly against the “vibe coding” moniker — but as I’ve pointed out, LLM tools will always gravitate towards being most useful to senior developers. It is also only devs with solid experience who can quickly evaluate whether a change is safe enough to entrust to an agent…

content.title: What Using AI Agents in DevOps Looks Like in 2026 - DuploCloud
content.text:
In a recent webinar session, Venkat Thiruvengadam, CEO and Founder of Duplocloud, and Vallinayagam Nallaperumal, Vice President of Strategic Technology and Cloud Operations at Metricstream, shared how agentic AI is reshaping the world of DevOps.

Join MetricStream and DuploCloud for a data-backed session exploring how agentic AI is reshaping DevOps practices to make teams faster, more compliant, and more resilient. It’s 2026, and DevOps teams are doing way more than writing scripts and reacting to tickets. They’re now building and managing AI agents to work alongside their AI engineers. This means they can continuously monitor systems and resolve issues quickly and efficiently. In a recent webinar session, Venkat Thiruvengadam, CEO and Founder of Duplocloud, and Vallinayagam Nallaperumal, Vice President of Strategic Technology and Cloud Operations at Metricstream, shared how agentic AI is reshaping the world of DevOps. They’re seeing teams get more proactive, faster at resolving issues, and better governed. Here, we take a peek into their conversation about what DevOps actually looks like with AI agents in 2026.

In 2026, that reactive mode is shifting. Now, instead of DevOps teams reacting to specific requirements, they build an AI engineer. Engineering teams can ask for something from DevOps, and DevOps can ask the LLM, an AI agent. ... So we’ve moved DevOps teams from writing scripts and constantly going back and forth to building the AI engineers that can do all that work for them, with access to all the knowledge on the internet. Now, your DevOps team is designing and supervising super intelligent system to execute tasks for them. They were scriptwriters. Now they’re orchestrators of AI talent. As AI transforms development workflows, it reshapes operations even more dramatically. ... It’s a step-by-step process you’re likely used to. AI agents introduce a continuous model. Instead of waiting for problems, agents monitor infrastructure in real time.

In 2026, DevOps looks different. It just does. AI agents are now able to continuously monitor environments, and engineers can interact conversationally with their infrastructure. Those agents can analyze tickets in mere minutes, and remediation is automated… but governed. We apply guardrails, and we contain drift before it spreads. DevOps teams are now defined by their ability to orchestrate intelligence. The shift, as we see it, is a structural one. And the organizations that embrace AI agents, paired with strong human oversight, are the ones building DevOps functions that are both faster and more proactive and better governed. Watch the full webinar here. ... Multicloud was supposed to give teams more flexibility, but instead it’s created tool sprawl, inconsistent security, and skyrocketing costs. DuploCloud... Read more · Learn how DevOps practices can strengthen cloud security.

In many cases, engineers don’t even need to write custom code to address common issues. With access to engineering, development, and operations data, AI agents can find the most likely solution quickly. This helps cut mean time to resolution (MTTR) and lets DevOps teams focus on higher-order challenges. The days of repetitive troubleshooting are starting to fade. As enterprises adopt AI agents, a critical question comes up over and over: how much should we build internally, and how much should we rely on vendors? The answer, of course, lies somewhere in the middle. Large language models are powerful knowledge engines. But they can’t safely execute changes inside your infrastructure on their own. An LLM can suggest a deployment command, but it can’t validate credentials, enforce permissions, or ensure guardrails are met. That’s where a middle orchestration layer becomes essential.

content.title: AI Predictions for 2026: A DevOps Engineer's Guide | Pulumi Blog
content.text:
Multiple agents working simultaneously need isolated, reproducible environments. More generated code means more artifacts to track, version, and deploy. A lot of people think that in the future, a single large language model will have a monopoly and be the best at absolutely everything. But what’s really going to happen is different providers will specialize and focus on being the best at different things. Google is going down the generalist route with Gemini, aiming to be the jack-of-all-trades. Anthropic is focusing on being the best for coding. You can see this in the benchmarks: when Opus 4.5 came out, the first benchmark they highlighted was for software engineering, because that’s what Anthropic is focusing on. Amazon is carving out its own niche with the Nova model family, announced at re:Invent 2025. The Nova 2 lineup includes specialized models: Pro for complex reasoning, Sonic for real-time voice conversations, and Omni for simultaneous text, audio, and video processing.

Agent-to-agent protocols are where AI agents operate in a peer network, discover each other’s capabilities in real time, and interact autonomously. When Google released their A2A protocol earlier this year, there was a ton of buzz. A lot of people thought it was going to be the next big standard, like the next MCP. But then it kind of fell to the wayside. The big reason is the chicken-and-egg problem. For A2A to be useful, you need many people to adopt it at the same time. Otherwise, if you build an A2A-compatible agent, it has no other agents to talk to. The whole value proposition is lost unless you already have a big network to attach to. But that’s finally changing. The Linux Foundation launched the A2A project in June 2025, and adoption is accelerating. Adobe, Microsoft, SAP, ServiceNow, and S&P Global are all implementing A2A. In July 2025, Google released version 0.3 of the A2A protocol with a more stable interface critical to accelerating enterprise adoption.

Cursor 2.0 lets you run up to eight agents in parallel on a single prompt, using git worktrees or remote machines to prevent file conflicts. Each agent operates in its own isolated copy of your codebase. AWS validated this direction at re:Invent 2025 by announcing “frontier agents” including Kiro for autonomous coding, along with dedicated security and DevOps agents. These agents maintain state, log actions, operate with policy guardrails, and integrate directly with CI/CD pipelines. For infrastructure specifically, Pulumi Neo represents this same shift. Instead of writing code or running CLI commands for every operation, you describe what you need in natural language and Neo handles the implementation. It works across your entire infrastructure, understanding dependencies and creating execution plans that go through pull requests for review. For DevOps engineers, this means your pipelines need to accommodate AI-generated code at scale.

Multiple agents working simultaneously need isolated, reproducible environments. More generated code means more artifacts to track, version, and deploy. A lot of people think that in the future, a single large language model will have a monopoly and be the best at absolutely everything. But what’s really going to happen is different providers will specialize and focus on being the best at different things. Google is going down the generalist route with Gemini, aiming to be the jack-of-all-trades. Anthropic is focusing on being the best for coding. You can see this in the benchmarks: when Opus 4.5 came out, the first benchmark they highlighted was for software engineering, because that’s what Anthropic is focusing on. Amazon is carving out its own niche with the Nova model family, announced at re:Invent 2025. The Nova 2 lineup includes specialized models: Pro for complex reasoning, Sonic for real-time voice conversations, and Omni for simultaneous text, audio, and video processing.

This goes really well with agent-to-agent protocols. You can create a peer network where you monetize your agents. They all leverage each other but make payments whenever they take advantage of another agent’s capabilities. Cryptocurrency is the perfect solution for this kind of machine-to-machine network because it enables micropayments to be made quickly and globally. The x402 protocol has achieved 156,000 weekly transactions with 492% growth since launching in May 2025. It’s now integrated with Anthropic’s MCP Protocol, Google Gemini, OpenAI Codex, and other platforms. Stablecoins like USDC enable charging per request, per service, or per second of usage at near-zero transaction costs, enabling payments as low as $0.001 per request. When we want to do a rigorous code review, traditionally, we look line by line at all the changes. But coding agents are getting to the point where they can prove their code works through artifacts.

content.text:

content.title: 6 Software Development and DevOps Trends Shaping 2026
content.text:
If things are messy, you might ship faster… into more problems. That’s why 2026 is about agents plus guardrails, not agents alone. If GitHub Copilot feels weak for our use case, there are solid open-source alternatives: Continue (open-source assistant for VS Code/JetBrains; we can connect different models and contexts, and it supports agent-style workflows) Tabby (open-source, self-hosted coding assistant, often positioned as an on-prem alternative to Copilot) And if we want “more agent, less IDE autocomplete,” these are worth a look: ... A semantic layer is a part of data architecture that translates complex data into business-friendly terms, so “revenue,” “active customer,” or “incident severity” mean the same thing everywhere. An ontology is a more formal version of that idea: a shared domain model with clear definitions and relationships (for example: Customer owns Contract, Contract relates to Product, Product has Region rules).

Cut alert noise and reduce MTTR in this live webinar on agentic AI. ... Boris Zaikin CORE · Jan. 06, 26 · Analysis ... Be the first to like this post! It looks like you're not logged in. Sign in to see who liked this post! ... Join the DZone community and get the full member experience. Join For Free · In 2025, many teams tried new things in software development and DevOps — AI copilots, new platforms, more automation, and more security checks. Some of it worked great, some of it created new mess (tool sprawl, unclear ownership, higher cloud bills, and “we ship faster but break more”). Heading into 2026, the focus is shifting from experimentation to ensuring reliability and repeatability. Leaders and practitioners are asking the same questions: How do we move fast without losing quality? How do we keep systems secure without slowing teams down?

Agentic AI is AI that can work toward a goal with limited supervision, by planning steps and using tools (not just generating text). Think: “take this issue, make a change, run checks, and prepare a pull request for review.” · Teams are overloaded by repetitive tasks around delivery — triage, updating configs, chasing flaky tests, fixing CI, writing PR summaries, and digging through logs. Agents can reduce that toil and shorten feedback loops, so engineers spend more time on decisions and design (and less time on copy-and-paste work). For example, GitHub documents workflows in which Copilot can be asked to create a pull request, with the developer approving it before it proceeds. But there’s a catch: AI tends to amplify what already exists in your engineering system. If your foundations are strong (good tests, clear standards, solid CI), you get faster.

If things are messy, you might ship faster… into more problems. That’s why 2026 is about agents plus guardrails, not agents alone. If GitHub Copilot feels weak for our use case, there are solid open-source alternatives: Continue (open-source assistant for VS Code/JetBrains; we can connect different models and contexts, and it supports agent-style workflows) Tabby (open-source, self-hosted coding assistant, often positioned as an on-prem alternative to Copilot) And if we want “more agent, less IDE autocomplete,” these are worth a look: ... A semantic layer is a part of data architecture that translates complex data into business-friendly terms, so “revenue,” “active customer,” or “incident severity” mean the same thing everywhere. An ontology is a more formal version of that idea: a shared domain model with clear definitions and relationships (for example: Customer owns Contract, Contract relates to Product, Product has Region rules).

As we use more AI assistants and agents in engineering and ops, they need trusted context to make safe decisions. Graph-based RAG approaches are gaining attention because they can combine text with relationships, not only similarity search. GraphRAG is one example of this direction. And to keep this domain model clean over time, we can validate graph data with constraint rules like SHACL, so “domain truth” doesn’t drift into chaos. Platform engineering is about building internal developer platforms (IDPs) — shared, self-service infrastructure and tools that help teams build, test, deploy, and operate software more consistently. Rather than each team reinventing its own pipeline, platform teams create golden paths (pre-approved, repeatable ways to get things done). As we move into 2026, these platforms are evolving beyond CI/CD automation into AI-ready platforms that embed intelligence, security, and observability into the developer experience.

content.title: A RedMonk Conversation: GitLab Duo, Agentic AI, and the SDLC in 2025 (with Emilio Salvador) – RedMonk
content.text:
Emilio Salvador, VP of DevRel & Strategy at GitLab, joins RedMonk’s Rachel Stephens to discuss current trends around agentic AI: how do agents differ from code assistants; how can AI come into play in the larger software development lifecycle ...

Emilio Salvador, VP of DevRel & Strategy at GitLab, joins RedMonk’s Rachel Stephens to discuss current trends around agentic AI: how do agents differ from code assistants; how can AI come into play in the larger software development lifecycle (beyond writing code); where is the “human in the loop” in all of this. Emilo and Rachel also recap some important announcements from GitLab in 2024–including the GitLab Duo Enterprise and AWS Q announced at re:Invent 2024–and discuss what we might expect to see from GitLab in 2025. This was a RedMonk video, sponsored by GitLab. ... Rachel Stephens: Hi, everyone. Welcome to RedMonk Conversations. I’m Rachel Stephens with RedMonk, and with me today, I have Emilio Salvador. Emilio has a background with pretty much all the CSPs, as far as I’m aware, but currently is a VP of DevRel and Strategy at GitLab.

Emilio Salvador, VP of DevRel & Strategy at GitLab, joins RedMonk’s Rachel Stephens to discuss current trends around agentic AI: how do agents differ from code assistants; how can AI come into …

Let’s start by just talking about where we’ve seen AI so far, which is AI assistance and coding assistance, specifically, versus AI agents, because I think that’s another thing that’s on the scene a lot right now. Emilio: Yeah, so let’s start with that. I think that we are now, as you said before, I mean, AI is here to stay. Everyone is using it. We may not want to see it, but it’s happening. And it’s changing the way we develop software. Why is that? I think because it’s helping develop us to streamline the entire process. I think it’s helping them free up time so they can truly devote their brain power to the things that matter the most, which is about solving problems. I’ve been a developer for many, many years. One of the challenges that we all face is that we had to do boring things all the time that were required, that anyone could do.

Can you please tell me what this thing is doing. Junior developers, more than anyone else, are benefiting the most from it. Why? Because all of a sudden, you jumped into tens of thousands of lines of code and you’re like, Oh, my God, what do I do with this? And AI is there to help you. It can summarize projects, entire projects in just a few seconds that will help you save time and start working very rapidly. But the challenge, precisely, it’s because those assistants are there. They are waiting for you to act. To ask a question, and they are not proactive. They are purely reactive. That is number one. What we have seen with agents is that we are transforming the way we interact with those assistants, and truly, they end up becoming peers, like another developer that is working alongside with you, and that can remove a ton of toil from your day-to-day work.

A single developer will be able to design, test, scan, and deploy applications that once required an entire team. Here, AI will take on the heavy lifting, like syntax, scaffolding, and optimization, while humans focus on architecture, validation, and strategic direction. Human oversight will remain critical before software reaches production, ensuring quality and compliance. This new era will mark the beginning of true agentic development, where AI agents collaborate with developers at every step, accelerating innovation while preserving human judgment. Moving beyond current single-task agents that write or debug code, a meta-Agent will act as project manager and chief architect.

content.title: Google Adds Deeper Context and Control for Agentic Developer Workflows
content.text:
Mitch Ashley, VP and Practice Lead, Software Lifecycle Engineering at Futurum, examines how Google’s Developer Knowledge API and Gemini CLI hooks externalize agent context and governance, shaping production-ready AI development and raising ...

Mitch Ashley, VP and Practice Lead, Software Lifecycle Engineering at Futurum, examines how Google’s Developer Knowledge API and Gemini CLI hooks externalize agent context and governance, shaping production-ready AI development and raising the bar for trusted agentic workflows.

Mitch Ashley, Keith Kirkpatrick, Fernando Montenegro, Nick Patience & Brad Shimmin ... VP and Practice Lead Fernando Montenegro shares insights from the Cisco AI Summit 2026, where leaders from the major AI ecosystem providers gathered to discuss bridging the AI ROI gap... ... Get important insights straight to your inbox, receive first looks at eBooks, exclusive event invitations, custom content, and more. We promise not to spam you or sell your name to anyone.

The News: In late January and early February 2026, Google announced two updates aimed at improving the reliability and enterprise readiness of AI-driven development tools. The Developer Knowledge API with the associated Model Context Protocol server entered public preview. The API exposes Google’s official developer documentation as a canonical, machine-readable source, re-indexed within roughly 24 hours of updates. The MCP server enables AI assistants, IDEs, and agents to retrieve and reason over the latest documentation at runtime using an open protocol. In a prior announcement, Google added hooks to Gemini CLI, its AI-powered command-line assistant. Hooks allow teams to execute custom logic at defined lifecycle events inside the agent loop. Hooks can inject context, enforce security and compliance checks, block sensitive actions, or automate workflow steps without modifying the core agent.

content.text:
When you move from thinking about reducing effort to expanding scope, only the most advanced agentic capabilities will do." Dohmke identifies seven essential skills for the AI era: 1) AI fluency - Understanding different AI tools and models · 2) Delegation and agent orchestration - Setting up AI for success · 3) Human-AI collaboration - Working with AI in real-time · 4) Fundamentals - Still need deep programming knowledge · 5) Verification and quality control - Checking AI's work rigorously · 6) Product understanding - Systems thinking beyond just code · 7) Architecture and systems design - Guiding AI integration · Importantly, he notes: "Vague or one-liner instructions were never enough to empower human colleagues to successfully hit goals, and they will not work for AI agents either." This aligns with his earlier warning that while AI tools can help beginners get started, "at some point, you'll run into limitations.

The most significant finding involves Stage 4 developers. "The developers at stage 4 unanimously declare their role has shifted," Dohmke writes. "They now focus on the delegation and the verification of a task." These developers no longer write code. Instead, they manage AI agents. "Delegation is about setting up the agents for success with rich context and instructions, designing and refining prompts, reviewing AI's plans and tradeoffs, and tweaking before proceeding," the research explains. "Verification is all about tearing down the agent's work – they review and validate that the AI-generated implementation fulfills the objectives and conventions it needs to." One developer captured this transformation: "Maybe we become less code producers and more code enablers. My next title might be Creative Director of Code." When GitHub asked about AI writing 90% of code, the timeline was shocking: Half of developers believe this will happen within 5 years ...

But crucially, Dohmke notes: "To them this future scenario did not feel like their value or identity is diminished, but that it is reinvented." On X, he emphasized: "Developers aren't worried. They're optimistic and realistic about the changes ahead." The research revealed something unexpected. "Developers rarely mentioned 'time saved' as the core benefit of working in this new way with agents. They were all about increasing ambition." As Dohmke put it on X: "Time savings? Sure. But the real shift is ambition. Developers are raising the ceiling, not just lowering the cost." This finding aligns with his previous observations. In a July podcast appearance, Dohmke noted that AI hasn't cleared company backlogs: "I haven't seen a single company say, 'We've finished everything on our backlog thanks to AI.' If anything, AI is creating more possibilities and more work."

This aligns with his earlier warning that while AI tools can help beginners get started, "at some point, you'll run into limitations.

Dohmke acknowledges resistance: "The software developer role is set on a path of significant change. Not everyone will want to make the change. Managing agents to achieve outcomes may sound unfulfilling to many." But he argues developers have always done this: "managing their computers via programming languages to achieve outcomes." "This shift isn't hypothetical. It's happening now," Dohmke wrote on X. His research shows "developers worldwide are already moving from skepticism to confidence, reshaping their roles, practices, and mindsets as they partner with AI." His message is clear: "This is no longer a question of productivity. It's a question of reinvention. The job of the software developer isn't disappearing. It's being reborn." # Most Influential Figures in Agentic DevOps — March 2026

> Researched: March 4, 2026. This covers practitioners, analysts, executives, and founders actively shaping the agentic DevOps conversation right now — not just general AI influencers.

---

## What "Agentic DevOps" Means Right Now

The space has coalesced around a clear definition: AI agents that don't just assist, but **reason, plan, and act autonomously** across the software delivery lifecycle — writing code, generating tests, remediating incidents, managing CI/CD pipelines, and making operational decisions in real time. The shift is from "Human-in-the-Loop" to "Human-on-the-Loop."

---

## Tier 1: Founders & Operators Building the Infrastructure

### Thomas Dohmke
**Founder & CEO, Entire | Former CEO, GitHub**

The single most significant operator in this space right now. Left GitHub in August 2025 after scaling Copilot and immediately launched **Entire** — a $60M seed-funded ($300M valuation) open-source developer platform built specifically for the era where agents write most of the code. The seed round is the largest in developer tools history.

His first product, **Checkpoints**, captures agent context (prompts, reasoning, decisions) as versioned Git data — solving the "review bottleneck" problem that emerges when agents produce code faster than humans can evaluate it. Entire supports Claude Code and Gemini CLI already.

**His thesis:** "Soon, developers won't look at the code anymore, as agents will write way more than humans can review." Headcount now includes tokens — not just salaries.

**Why he matters:** He didn't just watch GitHub Copilot happen — he ran the platform. Now he's building the meta-layer for agentic coding management. No one has more institutional knowledge of where developer tooling breaks at scale.

**Follow:** [@ashtom](https://github.com/ashtom) on GitHub, his Axios/TechCrunch interviews (Feb 2026)

---

### Venkat Thiruvengadam
**CEO & Founder, DuploCloud**

Actively redefining what DevOps looks like operationally in 2026. His framing: DevOps teams have evolved from "script writers" to **"orchestrators of AI talent."** Rather than writing scripts and going back and forth, teams now build AI engineers that do the work for them with access to full knowledge bases.

Strong focus on the "build vs. buy" orchestration layer debate and why a middle governance/compliance layer remains essential — LLMs can suggest, but they can't validate credentials or enforce permissions without that wrapper.

**Why he matters:** Real practitioner perspective on what's actually deploying in production, not theory.

---

## Tier 2: Analysts Driving the Narrative

### Mitch Ashley
**VP & Practice Lead, Software Lifecycle Engineering — The Futurum Group**

The most prolific analyst voice in agentic DevOps right now. 30+ years of background as entrepreneur, CTO, CIO, and head of engineering before becoming an analyst — so he's not just a commentator, he's been in the chair.

Hosts **"Agents of Dev"** (with Brad Shimmin), **DevOps Unbound**, and **CISO Talk**. Publishing regular research on agent-driven development, observability-native software, and what multi-agent execution actually looks like in practice. Recently covered GitHub's Agent HQ, Google's Developer Knowledge API and Gemini CLI hooks, Dynatrace Perform 2026, and Harness's Human-Aware Change Agent.

At the **Predict 2026** conference (DevOps.com), Ashley presented on how AI agents are collapsing development cycles, automating testing, and reshaping CI/CD — and what DevOps practices have to do to stay relevant.

**Why he matters:** He's covering this beat daily with practitioner credibility. If something significant happens in agentic DevOps tooling, he's writing about it within days.

**Follow:** [devops.com/author/mitchellashley](https://devops.com/author/mitchellashley), LinkedIn, Futurum Group

---

### Brad Shimmin
**Research Analyst, The Futurum Group**

Co-host of "Agents of Dev" with Mitch Ashley. Focused on the data layer of agentic systems — his line from Predict 2026: *"AI success depends on data,"* putting DevOps at the nexus of data pipelines and agentic flows. Covers semantic layers, data governance, and what it means to feed agentic systems well.

**Why he matters:** Covers the foundational data infrastructure that makes agentic DevOps reliable vs. chaotic. Increasingly relevant as the "garbage in, garbage out" problem surfaces in production agent deployments.

---

### Keith Kirkpatrick
**Principal Analyst, Futurum Group**

Focused specifically on how agentic AI **breaks SaaS pricing models**. As agents do work traditionally billed per-seat, the accountability and billing frameworks fall apart. This is a massive structural issue for DevOps tools vendors — and Kirkpatrick is the person most publicly working through the implications.

Presented at Predict 2026 specifically on this topic.

---

### Dion Hinchcliffe
**Distinguished Thought Leader, Futurum Group**

Senior CIO/CTO-lens analyst. His focus for 2026 is how AI workloads are stressing existing cloud and operational models — forcing CIOs to reinvent cloud strategy, security posture, and operational frameworks simultaneously.

**Why he matters for DevOps:** He's the bridge between C-suite strategy and infrastructure teams. Relevant if you're speaking to enterprise audiences about the organizational side of agentic adoption.

---

## Tier 3: Practitioners & Executives Setting Direction

### Emilio Salvador
**VP of Strategy & Developer Relations, GitLab**

One of the most quoted practitioners in 2026 DevOps predictions. His central forecast: **more than 75% of developers will be architecting, governing, and orchestrating rather than building applications by end of 2026.** Human verification becomes the most valuable skill in the delivery chain — teams will spend 60% of time on verification/quality gates vs. 20% on hands-on-keyboard coding.

Also predicts the rise of "meta-Agents" — AI systems that act as project managers, delegating to coder, tester, and DevOps sub-agents.

**Why he matters:** GitLab has a full-lifecycle view of agentic DevOps (plan → code → test → deploy → operate) via GitLab Duo. Salvador has the data from real enterprise usage at scale.

---

### Greg Benson
**CTO, SnapLogic**

Defined the next competitive battleground cleanly: *"In 2026, the competition will shift from 'how do you prompt a single agent?' to 'how do you coordinate multiple agents as a team?'"*

That framing has been widely cited in DevOps prediction coverage. Agent orchestration — not individual agent capability — is the hard problem.

---

### John Capobianco
**Head of Developer Relations, Selector**

Coined the term **"VibeOps"** — describing the shift from NetOps → DevOps → NetDevOps → AIOps → VibeOps, where digital coworkers participate in daily operations with agency through toolchains. His framing of MCP as "the USB-C of software" — enabling autonomous agents to plug into vast ecosystems — has gotten significant traction.

**Why he matters:** Bridges the network/ops side of DevOps (often underrepresented) with the agentic AI narrative.

---

### Nancy Wang
**SVP, Head of Engineering and AI, 1Password**

Predicting the rise of the developer-as-orchestrator model, particularly for low-code practitioners. Her angle is on how value shifts from code production to design and creative direction. Relevant because 1Password is an enterprise security product — her predictions carry practitioner weight on the DevSecOps dimension.

---

## Tier 4: Thought Leaders Worth Watching

| Name | Role | Why Relevant |
|------|------|-------------|
| **Fernando Montenegro** | VP Cybersecurity & Resilience, Futurum | Trust, governance, and DevSecOps in agentic pipelines |
| **Vidya Shankaran** | Field CTO, Commvault | Agent performance dashboards — "how many agents are working for us vs. against us" |
| **Ensar Seker** | CISO, SOCRadar | AI agents automating full SDLC; security context |
| **Marcus Holm** | Tech Monitor | "Self-Healing DevOps" — resilience as the new DevOps metric |
| **Gergely Orosz** | The Pragmatic Engineer | Independent voice; investor in Entire; developer community authority |

---

## What's Actually Moving the Space Right Now (March 2026)

1. **GitHub's Agentic Workflows** — "continuous AI" into CI/CD, announced Feb 2026
2. **AWS Frontier Agents** — Kiro (coding), plus dedicated security and DevOps agents, from re:Invent 2025
3. **Entire / Checkpoints** — Thomas Dohmke's $60M platform for managing AI-written code
4. **Pulumi Neo** — natural language infrastructure management, PR-based review
5. **MCP adoption** — Model Context Protocol as the agent interop standard
6. **A2A Protocol** — now backed by Linux Foundation, gaining enterprise adoption (Adobe, Microsoft, SAP, ServiceNow)
7. **Dynatrace Perform 2026** — domain-specific agents as observability-led agent control planes
8. **Harness Human-Aware Change Agent** — human-in-loop signals for agent-driven delivery

---

## The Core Tension Everyone Is Navigating

**Speed vs. governance.** Agents can collapse development cycles and automate testing, but:
- Organizations deploy agents faster than they can secure them
- Without strong engineering foundations (good tests, clear standards, solid CI), agents amplify the mess
- The companies winning in 2026 are implementing "bounded autonomy" — sandbox-first, human-on-the-loop, comprehensive audit trails

The role shift is real: scriptwriters → orchestrators. But the organizations that treat agents as productivity add-ons rather than structural transformation consistently fail to scale them.

---

*Sources: DevOps.com, Futurum Group, DEVOPSdigest, DuploCloud, The New Stack, TechCrunch, Axios, GeekWire, Pulumi Blog (March 2026)*

# The definitive interview list for agentic DevOps thought leaders

**Agentic DevOps — AI agents that autonomously execute CI/CD, infrastructure management, incident response, and deployment — became the defining paradigm of 2025–2026.** Microsoft formally branded the concept at Build 2025, and since then an entire ecosystem of founders, analysts, practitioners, and open source maintainers has coalesced around it. The 23 people below are the most influential and reachable voices across five categories, all verified active in 2025–2026, and all realistic interview targets for a DevOps thought leadership program. Every person was cross-checked across DevOps.com, The New Stack, KubeCon speaker lists, GitHub/GitLab blogs, Futurum Group, podcasts, LinkedIn, and Twitter/X.

---

## Founders and CEOs building the agentic DevOps stack

These are the builders — shipping products, raising capital, and evangelizing a thesis. All are media-active and incentivized to do interviews.

### 1. Solomon Hykes — Co-Founder & CEO, Dagger

| Detail | Info |
|---|---|
| **Why agentic DevOps** | Created Docker; now building the container runtime layer for agentic CI/CD. Shipped **Container Use** (open-source MCP server giving AI agents isolated containerized environments) and native LLM primitives in Dagger v0.18+. Argues traditional CI/CD will disappear because agents develop 100x faster than humans. |
| **2025–2026 output** | Launched Container Use; appeared on the **Agentic DevOps podcast** ("Agentic CI/CD with Solomon Hykes"), Heavybit's Open Source Ready, dbt's Analytics Engineering Podcast, InfoQ Podcast, MLOps Community; spoke at KubeCon and Civo Navigate. |
| **Public presence** | LinkedIn: [linkedin.com/in/solomonhykes](https://linkedin.com/in/solomonhykes) · Twitter/X: [@solomonstre](https://x.com/solomonstre) · GitHub: github.com/shykes |
| **Unique thesis** | "Docker containerized the app; Dagger containerizes the factory that builds the app." Agents need programmable, repeatable, modular environments — containers are the isolation primitive for agents, just as they were for microservices. Warns about "gatekeeping" in the MCP ecosystem. |
| **Reachability** | **High** — Prolific podcast guest (5+ in 2025 alone), accepts invitations from shows of all sizes, legendary DevOps name. |

### 2. Amit Eyal Govrin — Co-Founder & CEO, Kubiya.ai

| Detail | Info |
|---|---|
| **Why agentic DevOps** | Built an **agentic engineering platform** with AI Teammates that DevOps teams delegate tasks to via Slack/Teams/CLI. Shipped Captain Kubernetes (autonomous K8s operations agent) and Kubiya Composer (deterministic agent execution). Key differentiator: **same input = same output, every time** with zero-trust security. |
| **2025–2026 output** | Unveiled Enterprise AI Stack for Agents at **KubeCon London** (Apr 2025); on-stage theCUBE interview at **KubeCon NA** (Nov 2025); launched Kubiya Composer on Product Hunt (Jun 2025). Armon Dadgar (HashiCorp co-founder) as advisor. |
| **Public presence** | LinkedIn: [linkedin.com/in/amiteyalgovrin](https://linkedin.com/in/amiteyalgovrin) · 3+ theCUBE video interviews · KubeCon regular · Press: SiliconANGLE, Calcalist, Technology Innovators |
| **Unique thesis** | **"Delegation is the new automation."** Traditional automation fails because it requires extensive planning and maintenance — a "time-to-automation paradox." Coined **Intent-Based Operations**: contextualizing human intent using natural language. |
| **Reachability** | **High** — Media-friendly seed-stage CEO with strong incentive for visibility, multiple video interviews, KubeCon regular. |

### 3. Spiros Xanthos — Founder & CEO, Resolve AI

| Detail | Info |
|---|---|
| **Why agentic DevOps** | Building an **autonomous SRE** — multi-agent AI that maps production topology, reasons across logs/metrics/deployments, and autonomously resolves incidents. Co-created **OpenTelemetry** (massive community credibility). Reports **70%+ reduction in MTTR** and 20 hours saved per engineer weekly. |
| **2025–2026 output** | Raised **$35M Seed** (Oct 2024) then **Series A at $1B valuation** from Lightspeed (Dec 2025). Angel investors include Jeff Dean, Fei-Fei Li, Reid Hoffman, and Thomas Dohmke. Stanford presentation on multi-agent architecture. Greylock GreyMatter long-form interview. |
| **Public presence** | LinkedIn: [linkedin.com/in/spiros](https://linkedin.com/in/spiros) · Featured in TechCrunch, Greylock GreyMatter, SiliconANGLE · Previously: Log Insight → VMware, Omnition → Splunk |
| **Unique thesis** | "The hardest part of software engineering isn't writing code — it's running production." AI coding assistants accelerate code production but make operational complexity *worse*. You need multi-agent systems with deep production topology understanding. |
| **Reachability** | **High** — Serial entrepreneur experienced with press; company in active growth mode. Podcast "get" for DevOps shows — he hasn't done many yet. |

### 4. Jyoti Bansal — Co-Founder & CEO, Harness

| Detail | Info |
|---|---|

## Five strategic themes for compelling interviews

The research revealed five converging narratives that cut across all 23 people — any interview series structured around these themes would produce distinctive content.

**The "outer loop" bottleneck is the real story.** AI coding assistants solved the easy problem (writing code). The hard problem — testing, security, deployment, governance, incident response — is where agentic DevOps lives. Bansal, Xanthos, and Zachary are the strongest voices here. Pairing a founder (Bansal) with a practitioner (Zachary) on this theme would produce a high-contrast conversation.

**MCP is the connective tissue, and it's also the security risk.** David Soria Parra, Matyushentsev, and Viktor Farcic all see MCP as the enabling standard, but Guy Podjarny's team (Simon Maple specifically) has flagged serious security vulnerabilities in connecting AI agents to GitHub via MCP. This tension — enablement vs. exposure — makes for compelling debate.

**"Progressive autonomy" is the emerging enterprise playbook.** Srikanth Rajan at Salesforce and Vijay Samuel at eBay both independently arrived at the same conclusion: start human-in-the-loop, then gradually increase agent autonomy as confidence builds. This practitioner consensus contrasts with the founder enthusiasm of Hykes ("CI/CD will disappear") and Govrin ("delegation is the new automation").

**Platform engineering is the prerequisite, not the afterthought.** Zachary (Thoughtworks), Sandoval (Adobe), and Levine (Solo.io) all emphasize that organizations without solid platform foundations will see agents fail faster, not succeed faster. Rickey Zachary's line — "AI doesn't fix broken systems; it just breaks them faster" — is the most quotable insight in the entire landscape.

**The DevOps godfather is back.** Patrick Debois coining DevOps in 2009 and now working full-time on AI agents for the software delivery lifecycle is itself a narrative arc. His collaboration with Guy Podjarny at Tessl, combined with his extraordinary recent publication output (7+ articles in January–February 2026 alone), signals that the person who started the DevOps movement sees agentic AI as its most significant evolution. An interview with Debois framed as "the future of DevOps from the person who named it" would be a signature piece of content.

---

## Recommended outreach priority

For Michael Forrester's interview program, the following sequencing maximizes impact while building momentum:

**Start with the most reachable high-value targets**: Bret Fisher (natural collaboration partner — he runs the Agentic DevOps podcast), Viktor Farcic (independent, collaborative, weekly audience), and Engin Diri (community engineer whose job is engagement). These three will likely say yes quickly and establish the series.

**Then go for the founder tier**: Solomon Hykes (legendary name, proven podcast guest), Amit Eyal Govrin (media-hungry seed-stage CEO), and Adam Jacob (another DevOps legend pivoting to agents). All three are accessible and have distinctive theses.

**Land the practitioners for credibility**: Vijay Samuel (eBay — no-hype message), Srikanth Rajan (Salesforce — 1,400 clusters), and Rickey Zachary (Thoughtworks — "AI breaks broken systems faster"). These interviews provide the hands-on war stories audiences crave.

**Then pursue the prestige gets**: Patrick Debois (the "godfather" angle), Mitch Ashley (analyst perspective), and David Soria Parra (MCP creator). Finally, Kelsey Hightower remains the dream booking — selective but still active on the conference circuit through Civo, and his pragmatic contrarian take ("an AI agent is software — we're back to square one") would make for an unforgettable episode.
